concepts.title: Theorem on inverse of a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Inverse or reciprocal of a square matrix

Let $A$ be a square matrix of order $n$. Then a matrix $B$ (if such a matrix exists) is called the inverse of $A$ if $AB=BA={{I}_{n}}$ Inverse of the square matrix $ ext{A}$ is denoted by ${{\mathbf{A}}^{-1}}$

Existence of the inverse:

The inverse of a square matrix $ ext{A}$ exists if and only if $ ext{A}$ is a non-singular matrix.

If part: Let $ ext{A}$ be non-singular square matrix of order $ ext{n}$. Then $| ext{A}| eq 0$

Let $B= rac{\operatorname{adj}.A}{|A|}$

Then $AB= rac{A(\operatorname{adj}A)}{|A|}= rac{|A|{{I}_{n}}}{|A|}={{I}_{n}}\left[ ecause A\cdot (\operatorname{adj}A)=|A|{{I}_{n}} ight]\ldots (1)$

Hence B i.e. $ rac{\operatorname{adj}.\mathbf{A}}{|\mathbf{A}|}$ is the inverse of matrix $\mathbf{A}$ (by definition of inverse)

Only if part: Let $A$ be a square matrix of order $n$. Let inverse of $A$ exist. Let $B$ be the inverse of $A$. Then by definition of inverse

$ egin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & ext{AB}={{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}}\Rightarrow | ext{AB}|=| ext{In}|=1 \ ext{ or }\quad & | ext{A}|| ext{B}|=1[ herefore | ext{AB}|=| ext{A}|| ext{B}|] \ herefore & | ext{A}| e 0, ext{ because product }| ext{A}|| ext{B}| ext{ is non-zero} ext{. } \ \end{array}$

Hence $ ext{A}$ is non singular.

Note: (i) ${{ ext{A}}^{-1}}= rac{\operatorname{adj}\cdot ext{A}}{| ext{A}|}$ (ii) $ ext{A}{{ ext{A}}^{-1}}={{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}}[$ From (1)]

Theorems: 

(i) If $ ext{A}$ and $ ext{B}$ be any two non-singular matrices, then $ ext{AB}$ is also a non-singular matrix and ${{(AB)}^{-1}}={{B}^{-1}}{{A}^{-1}}$.

$ herefore \quad ext{A}, ext{B}$ are non-singular $ herefore | ext{A}| e 0,|~ ext{B}| e 0$

$ herefore \quad | ext{AB}|=| ext{A}| ext{B}\mid e 0$ Hence $ ext{AB}$ is non-singular.

Now $AB\left( {{B}^{-1}}{{A}^{-1}} ight)$

$\left. =A\left\{ B\left( {{B}^{-1}}{{A}^{-1}} ight) ight\}=A\{{{B}^{-1}}){{A}^{-1}} ight\}[ ext{ by associative law }]$

$=A\left\{ {{ ext{I}}_{n}}{{A}^{-1}} ight\}\{ herefore ext{B}{{ ext{B}}^{-1}}={{I}_{n}}]$

$= ext{A}{{ ext{A}}^{-1}}\left[ herefore {{ ext{I}}_{n}}~{{ ext{A}}^{-1}}={{ ext{A}}^{-1}} ight]$

$={{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}}$

Hence ${{B}^{-1}}{{A}^{-1}}$ is the inverse of A B

$ herefore \quad {{( ext{AB})}^{-1}}={{ ext{B}}^{-1}}~{{ ext{A}}^{-1}}$

(ii) If$ ext{A}$ is a non singular matrix, then ${{\left( {{ ext{A}}^{-1}} ight)}^{-1}}= ext{A}$

Let $A$ be a square matrix of order $n$,

Then ${{ ext{A}}^{-1}}~ ext{A}= ext{In}$ $ herefore \quad $ inverse of ${{ ext{A}}^{-1}}= ext{A}\quad herefore {{\left( {{ ext{A}}^{-1}} ight)}^{-1}}= ext{A}$

(iii) $ ext{I}_{ ext{n}}^{-1}={{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}}$ as $ ext{I}_{ ext{n}}^{-1}{{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}}={{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}}$

concepts.title: Finding inverse from row transformation
concepts.conceptBody:
Elementary operations of a matrix

Any of the following operations is called an elementary transformation.

(i) Interchanging any two rows of the given matrix. This transformation is indicated by${{\text{R}}_{\text{ij}}}$, if the ith

row are interchanged.

or denoted by ${{\text{R}}_{\text{i}}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{j}}}$

(ii) The multiplying every element of any row of the given matrix by a non-zero number. This transformation is indicated by ${{R}_{i}}(k)$, if the multiplication of the ith row by a constant $k(k\ne 0)$

or $\text{denoted by }{{\text{R}}_{\text{i}}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{k}\cdot {{\text{R}}_{\text{j}}}$

(iii) Addition of a constant multiple of the elements of any row to the corresponding elements of any other row. This transformation is indicated by${{R}_{ij}}(k)$, if the addition of the ith row to the elements of the $j$ th row multiplied by constant $k(k\ne 0)$.

or $\quad $ denoted by ${{\text{R}}_{\text{i}}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{R}}_{\text{i}}}+\text{k}{{\text{R}}_{\text{j}}}$

Note: Similarly, we can deline the three column operations. ${{\text{C}}_{\text{ij}}}\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{i}}}\leftarrow {{\text{C}}_{\text{j}}} \right)\cdot {{\text{C}}_{\text{i}}}(\text{k})\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{i}}}\to \text{k}{{\text{C}}_{\text{i}}} \right)$ and ${{\text{C}}_{\text{ij}}}(\text{k})\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{i}}}\to {{\text{C}}_{\text{i}}}+\text{k}{{\text{C}}_{\text{j}}} \right)$.

To compute the Inverse of a Matrix from elementary Row Transformation:

If $\text{A}$ is reduced to I by elementary row (L.H.S.) transformation, then suppose I is reduced to $\text{P}$ (R.H.S.) and not change $\text{A}$ in R.H.S.

i.e., $\text{A }=\text{IA}$

After transformation I = PA

then $\text{P}$ is the inverse of $\text{A}$

$\therefore \quad $ $\text{P}={{\text{A}}^{-1}}$

examples.title: If a matrix $A$ is such that $3 A^3+2 A^2+5 A+I=0$, then its inverse is
